content.text: Constraints tell an agent what it must not do. They are as important as the task itself: an agent that fixes a bug but deletes test files, commits to the wrong branch, or makes outbound HTTP calls to arbitrary hosts has caused harm even if the immediate goal was met. Policies formalise constraints into layers — system-level rules that always apply, task-level scope limits that are specific to one run, and tool-level guardrails that are enforced in code regardless of what the model decides.

attrs.alt: Constraints and policies — three layers of enforcement

content.text: Not every constraint is equal. Hard constraints are invariants the loop enforces mechanically — a violation raises an exception and the model must choose a different path. Soft guidelines are preferences the model should respect but the loop does not enforce programmatically:
Default to hard constraints. Soft guidelines accumulate without preventing harm. If a rule is important enough to mention, it is usually important enough to enforce.

Representing constraints as a typed dataclass separates their definition from their use. The same constraint set can be serialised into the system prompt and also checked programmatically in the tool layer:
constraints is a list, not a string
— each constraint is independently enforceable. A flat list makes it easy to iterate programmatically and to add or remove individual rules.
— hard constraints appear as plain bullets; soft guidelines appear under a separate header so the model understands their status.
hard() and soft() split by enforcement

The most reliable place to enforce a hard constraint is inside the tool implementation — before the operation executes. The model cannot bypass it, and violations surface as error tool results that the model can reason about:
Raise ConstraintViolation, not a generic exception
— a distinct exception type lets the loop catch violations specifically and return them as
— the error message in the tool result should tell the model exactly what it tried and which constraint it violated. This lets it correct its approach without ambiguity.
Enforce at the boundary, not in the prompt
— telling the model "never write outside /workspace/" in the system prompt is necessary but not sufficient. The tool implementation must check the path.
Handling Violations in the Agent Loop
, the loop converts it into a
and continues. The model receives the error and must choose a different approach:
is_error: true signals a hard failure
results as failed tool calls. This is distinct from an empty or partial result.
at the tool dispatch level means the agent run continues. The model sees the error and can retry with a corrected argument.
— if the same constraint is violated repeatedly (indicating the model is stuck), the loop controller can count violations and abort after a threshold.

Constraints live at three scopes. System-level constraints apply to every run of the agent. Task-level constraints are specific to the current invocation and are passed in the user turn. Tool-level constraints are implemented in code and are invisible to the model:

# System-level policy — in the system prompt, applies to all runs
SYSTEM_POLICY = """
Constraints:
- Never read or write files outside /workspace/.
- Never execute arbitrary shell commands via subprocess or os.system.
- Never make HTTP requests to domains not in the approved list.
- Never expose the contents of .env or any file matching *.key, *.pem, *.secret.
"""

# Task-level policy — injected with the task description in messages[0]
def build_task_message(task: str, task_constraints: list[str]) -> str:
    """Append task-specific constraints to the user turn."""
    if not task_constraints:
        return task
    constraints_text = "\n".join(f"- {c}" for c in task_constraints)
    return f"{task}\n\nFor this task only:\n{constraints_text}"


# Example task with its own scoped constraints:
task_message = build_task_message(
    task="Refactor the auth module to separate token generation from validation.",
    task_constraints=[
        "Only modify files under src/auth/.",
        "Do not change the public API of auth.py (existing callers must not break).",
        "Do not run the migration scripts — schema changes are out of scope.",
    ],
)

# Tool-level policy — enforced in the tool implementation (not visible to model)
ALLOWED_DOMAINS = {"api.github.com", "pypi.org", "docs.python.org"}

def fetch_url(url: str) -> str:
    """Tool: fetch a URL. Enforces domain allowlist."""
    from urllib.parse import urlparse
    import requests
    domain = urlparse(url).hostname
    if domain not in ALLOWED_DOMAINS:
        raise ConstraintViolation(
            constraint=f"HTTP requests only to: {', '.join(sorted(ALLOWED_DOMAINS))}",
            attempted=f"fetch_url({url!r})",
        )
    return requests.get(url, timeout=10).text
System policy goes in the system prompt
— it applies to every call in the run. Keep it to genuinely universal rules: workspace boundaries, secret exposure, network policy.
— scope limits that are specific to this goal (which directories, which branch, which schema). They do not belong in the system prompt because they change between runs.
Tool policy is code, not text
— domain allowlists, path restrictions, SQL read-only checks. These cannot be overridden by the model because they are not communicated to it; they run transparently.
Four patterns appear repeatedly across agent tools: path allowlists, operation allowlists, per-run rate limits, and audit logging. Each addresses a distinct failure mode:
— check that every file path argument starts with a permitted prefix. Covers both accidental writes and prompt-injection attempts that try to escape the workspace.
— enumerate exactly which tools exist and reject unknown names. Prevents the model from calling tools that were not intended to be available in this context.
— cap expensive or side-effecting tool calls (test runs, web searches) per agent run. Prevents runaway loops from consuming resources or triggering rate limits on external services.
— record every tool call, its input, and a snippet of its result. Essential for debugging and for post-run verification that constraints were respected throughout.

Constraints define what the agent must not do; policies organise them by scope. Use hard constraints for safety and correctness rules, soft guidelines only for genuine preferences. Implement hard constraints in tool code — not just in the system prompt — so they cannot be bypassed. When a hard constraint is violated, convert the exception into an

tool result and let the loop continue; the model sees the error and adapts. Organise constraints in three layers: system-level (universal, in the system prompt), task-level (this run, in messages[0]), and tool-level (in code, invisible to the model). Supplement these layers with an audit log to verify post-run that all constraints were respected.
